sanskrit_iast:
saktāḥ karmaṇy avidvāṁso
yathā kurvanti bhārata |
kuryād vidvāṁs tathāsaktaś
cikīrṣur loka-saṅgraham || 25 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.025 = MBh_06,025.025). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: As the ignorant perform their duties with attachment to results, the learned may similarly act, but without attachment, for the sake of leading people on the right path.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A person in Kṛṣṇa consciousness and a person not in Kṛṣṇa consciousness are differentiated by different desires. A Kṛṣṇa conscious person does not do anything which is not conducive to development of Kṛṣṇa consciousness.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.25 O scion of the Bharata dynasty, yatha, as; some avidvamsah, unenlightened poele; kurvanti, act. saktah, with attachment; karmani, to work, (thinking) ‘The reward of this work will accrue to me’; tatha, so; should vidvan, the enlightened person, the knower of the Self; kuryat,
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: saktāḥ karmaṇi avidvāṁsaḥ — the unwise, attached to action — yathā kurvanti — as they perform, with full energy, full surface-completeness — kuryāt vidvān tathā — the wise should perform, in just that manner — but asaktaḥ — without attachment — cikīrṣuḥ loka-saṅgraham — desirous of holding the world together. The verse therefore prescribes a same-surface-different-engine model. The wise actor's outer action looks identical to the unwise actor's outer action; the entire difference sits in the absence of attachment and the presence of loka-saṅgraha intent.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.25 ‘The ignorant’ are those people who do not know the entire truth about the self; ‘attached to their work’ means they are inseparably yoked to work.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the verse establishes that the wise actor must act with the same energy and the same visible discipline as the unwise actor — yathā kurvanti, tathā kuryāt — because any visible slackening would itself become the populace's pattern (per 3.21-3.23). The difference is purely in inner intention: cikīrṣuḥ loka-saṅgraham, desirous of the welfare of the world, not desirous of fruit. The verse names the realised actor's mode as outer-fullness with inner-detachment.

positions.rendering: As the ignorant perform their duties with attachment to results, the learned may similarly act, but without attachment, for the sake of leading people on the right path
Just as ignorant people act with attachment, the wise should act without attachment, intending the welfare of the world
the wise actor should engage in karma in just the way the unwise do — with full diligence and surface-completeness — but free of attachment, for loka-saṅgraha specifically

the verse asks the realised actor to perform karma yathā kurvanti bhārata — exactly as the unwise perform — so that no visible difference distracts the watchers; the only difference is the inner intention, which is loka-saṅgraha

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators stress yathā ... tathā as a same-surface comparison: the wise actor should act EXACTLY as the unwise actor, without difference visible to outside observers. Translators tend to soften this by adding qualifiers like 'similarly' or 'in like manner.' The verse's force is stronger: the realised one's action is not visibly distinct, even in zeal; only the engine differs.

literal_meaning: As the unwise act, attached to action, O Bhārata, so the wise should act, unattached, intending to maintain loka-saṅgraha (the welfare of the world).
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots from his own example back to the prescription for the realised actor. The structure is precise: the unwise (avidvāṁsaḥ) act because of attachment; the wise (vidvān) should act in the same surface-action but without attachment — for the express purpose (cikīrṣur) of loka-saṅgraha. The verse is the most direct prescription in the chapter: the realised one's action should be visibly identical to the unrealised one's action; only the inner motivation differs.
